title: Soundarya Chidambaram
text:
Teaching Philosophy, Pedagogy, and Courses Taught 

My research pays rigorous attention to research design, case selection and comparative case studies, and uses qualitative methods such as ethnographic fieldwork, in-depth interviews and participant observation, in addition to using the quantitative toolkit such as large-n data analysis.
These research themes and methodological approaches form the basis of the courses I teach:

Intro to Comparative Politics
Politics of India/South Asia
Senior Seminar on Comparative Ethnic Politics
Qualitative Research Methods
Globalization and its Critics

title: Can you teach an old dog new tricks?!
text:
Apparently, yes!
I participated in the incredible two-week 2025 Summer Institute in Computational Social Science at Carnegie Mellon University!
We went from wrangling data and viz in R, running topic models, and mapping networks to wrapping up with a fast-paced group project where we put our newly acquired skills to the test (and were awarded Runner-Up 🏆!!)
Huge thanks to the organizers and the amazing lineup of instructors, speakers, co-learners, and TAs for making SICSS-CMU a space to learn, experiment, and have fun with the messy magic of data + society.
Next stop: figuring out how to use these “new tricks” to answer big questions about politics, power, and people.

title: Soundarya Chidambaram
text:
Welcome to my homepage!

I’m Soundarya Chidambaram, Associate Professor of Political Science at Bucknell University.
I am a comparative political scientist studying religious nationalism, populism, welfare, civil society, and urban governance in India and South Asia. My research examines how marginalized communities navigate politics and public service provision in urban informal settlements. I combine ethnographic fieldwork, in-depth interviews, archival research, surveys, and mixed methods to understand the everyday politics of democracy and development.
My teaching draws heavily on these research experiences. Whether we’re discussing populism, ethnic conflict, economic development, or democratic backsliding, I encourage students to critically engage with politics beyond the Global North and to appreciate the richness and diversity of political life across South Asia and the wider Global South.
While my research has traditionally been grounded in qualitative methods, I am currently exploring computational social science methods such as text analysis, network analysis, and machine learning to explore new and old questions in comparative politics. This website reflects that journey. Alongside information about my research, publications, and teaching, you’ll find my computational social science sandbox where I experiment with R and Python.
Whether you’re here to learn about my research, browse teaching materials, or join me in exploring computational approaches to political science, I’m glad you’re here.
Download my CV
Curious what happens when a newbie coder gets loose with code? Head to Sandy’s GPT for the chaos! Don’t forget to check out my Publications page.

title: Soundarya Chidambaram
text:
My research explores the conditions under which religious nationalism, authoritarian populism, and divisive politics gain traction in democratic societies. Rather than focusing solely on elections, I examine the social movements, civil society organizations, and everyday political processes that enable populist leaders to build and sustain political support. Much of this work centers on India, where I study the evolution of Hindu nationalism as a grassroots movement and its implications for democracy, state-society relations, and majoritarian politics.
I also study patronage, welfare, and urban governance in marginalized communities across India. Drawing on extensive ethnographic fieldwork in urban informal settlements, I argue that everyday needs for public services—not simply ideological commitments—shape political participation and support for grassroots right-wing organizations. My research demonstrates how uneven state capacity influences political mobilization, particularly among women, helping explain variation in access to public services as well as the surprising success of Hindu nationalist organizations among the urban poor.
More recently, I have begun integrating computational social science into my research. I am exploring how text analysis, social network analysis, machine learning, and digital trace data can complement ethnographic and qualitative methods to study political rhetoric, social movements, polarization, and civic engagement in South Asia.
